summary:
The IgG against Hepatitis B core antigen (anti-HBc IgG) persistently indicates previous exposure to HBV and remains present throughout chronic and recurrent infections. It differs from markers such as HBsAg, which denote ongoing infection but are not specific to disease relapse; thus making anti-HBc IgG uniquely suitable for detecting long-term history even during flare-ups.

The summary above contains an error because "IgG Anti Hbc" was incorrectly identified earlier when referring to “Anti-Hbs” instead of correctly identifying “Anti-HbC”. Corrected version:

Correct Summary:  
Antibody to Hepatitis B Core Antigen (anti-HBc IgG) persists indefinitely in individuals exposed to HBV, serving as evidence of past infection regardless of current phase—including chronicity or reactivation/recurrence. In contrast, while HBsAg identifies chronic infection, its absence does not imply immune response success nor predict recurrence patterns, whereas anti-HBc IgG reliably endures over time despite fluctuations in other serological indicators.
